speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four-- Vera Eden, June 20th, 1996. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there we are in front of a SS. He was standing on a podium. And of course, the SS were so visible. It was-- I mean, this was truly crawling with them, screaming with their-- as I said, with everything-- armed to their teeth like if we could do something to them. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there we are. And he looked at my sister, inspected her from-- he had the most piercing look at you. You felt like, please, you know, take me away from here. Well, that was for sure. If I could have been taken that way from there then, that would have been wonderful. But you know, nobody could. There was no way, no way. So many people had to go through this cattle-- congregation, and nobody could rebel, which is so unbelievable in this day and age. But it's true. Nobody could.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there we are in front of Mengele, which we later learned. He looks at my sister. He looked at my mother. And he stopped at me. And he stopped at me, and he kept on looking at me. And at that point, my mother said-- no, excuse me-- he said, how old is she? And my mother said, 16, and she can work hard. Now, that moment was a moment when my life was decided, I-- I feel, because he let-- he looked me over once more and sent the three of us to the right side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we are walking with a group of people which we do not know. I do not know how that happened, but the group which we were with from Theresienstadt wasn't now-- nowhere. We were not in that group. There must have been another transport, which was intermingled, and I do-- and all languages were spoken. And I tell you, we clung together-- the three of us-- very tight. And we were stripped naked from the clothes we were wearing and taken into a room where they shaved us, and I mean shaved us everywhere-- our heads, everywhere where there was hair, that was shaven. My mother was on my right. My sister was on my left, or vice versa. I don't know. But one minute, you have got people next to you, and the next minute, you've got something which is supposed to be called a human being. Well, it is not. I am sorry to be so vain, but when somebody goes and shaves you head completely and-- and you are naked, and the SS is running through like if there was pieces of wood around, you lose a complete identity. You don't-- you-- you-- you stop being a human being. It has got something to do with the hair, yes. But with the men running around and slapping this and slapping that, which was-- nobody spoke, trust me. Nobody spoke. And from there, we went into a shower room, which was like a barn with the shower heads in line. And they called it delicing. And that's where all of a sudden became a rumor that there is gas going to come from the shower heads.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not believe it. Would you think that that would be true? I did not believe it. But you were-- I was young, and people did believe it. And the hysteria was just enormous. I mean, people screamed. They clawed to the top of the wall, which there was nowhere to get to because there was just a tiny window with-- with rails covering it so that, even if you were lucky enough to crawl there, what would you do? They would shoot you anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, it was-- this rumor started to right then and there, and we got water. We got water. We got-- at the other end-- you went through one end. You got out through the another end, and they threw at you the filthy uniform-- no shoes, no hat. And they marched us, and they distributed us through barracks. Again, some went there, and some went there. And you-- the three of us stayed together, but we were in a barrack, which was already occupied by-- by women only. The whole was women. And we were allotted a middle bunk for the three of us and one blanket. And we were exhausted. Let's put it this way-- just absolutely exhausted. And I think I must have fallen asleep just about right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at night, of course, the kapos-- I don't know if you heard about the kapos. They were a menace. They were a human disgrace. Of course, they saved their lives, so who am I to judge?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the SS at night would come into barracks and choose young girls to be entertained. And my mother, as I said before, was very observant. And when she saw what was supposed to be happening or imagined what was happening, she covered us with that one blanket, and that was it. The girls who were chosen came back in the morning, and they were committing suicide-- you know, walking into the barbed wire fence and, I mean, that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we were called out for appell, which we didn't work in Auschwitz. We were on appells, and you would stand for hours at attention in the cold, under-dressed, not fed, of course. I don't even remember having any food in Auschwitz-- maybe some bread, which was distributed in the barracks. But as such, to go for some food or had a utensil to eat with like we used to, I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, the appells had to be and were and the SS commandant would call out few from the row, and you haven't-- you would never see them again. And then you were taken back to the barracks, and so on and so forth. And we were not long in Auschwitz because the war was already-- the Russian front was coming, and we could hear the-- the-- the-- the bombing or whatever it was-- the cannons-- at night. It would light in the skies. You could hear it. And they were panicking. They were really incinerating, and the stink was something awful. And you know, I still did not believe that-- I saw the chimneys. I saw the smoke. I saw the people disappear. And I still felt that's not possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you inquire about your father at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Auschwitz, no. There was nowhere to acquire. I mean, nobody spoke to you. You were not spoken to unless you were kicked, slapped, or something like that. Anyhow, the psychological game of the showers repeated itself twice or three times a day. And by that time, you-- people knew about the gas, and they would look up the showers, and they prayed. I don't believe. I-- if-- we will not go into that subject. That would be too long. But either water or gas. Well, my group, which I was with, got always the water. And now we are in Auschwitz. This is what Auschwitz was-- emotional psychological warfare-- because you did not know what is going to happen and how-- well, how probably, yes, but when. It was a guessing game. And I think the grownups felt it much more than I did. Then we were called up-- a certain group. It must have been end of December. We were taken to the famous railway track, put on-- into a railway car, which was a car like normal people go in. We were-- it wasn't a long train, and we were squeezed in, but at least you were sitting on a bench. And we went. We didn't know where, and we went. Mother and my sister stayed together, and the rest we did not know. We did not know a soul. And we never encountered the people which we started from Theresienstadt. And we went on this train. I do not know how long. It wasn't-- and we stopped in a small station. They got us out. Now we are under the Wehrmacht. They are waiting there for us, and obviously, that was a Wehrmacht who was unfit for the-- for the front-- old guys, cold, under-dressed, everything. And we were taken-- it was about 200 of us approximately-- through a village, which was quite picturesque actually, if you want to go into-- into something. And you could see that the people were looking through their nice little windows-- curtains-- looking at these horrible looking things which were coming through their village. And we finished at the end of the village in a barn, which was equipped with bunks and a huge latrine. And the commandant of that-- it was a labor camp-- we were digging ditches in the forest in Kurzbach in Germany in-- it's a very forest-- geographically, full of forests. And we were housed in this barn. We were under the Wehrmacht, which was watching us. And we were under the commandant, who was a Wehrmacht commandant. Now, it sounds ridiculous, but that saved us from-- well, me and my sister, for sure. My mother-- that was, being older, and-- we'll come to that. But we worked during the day. We were digging ditches from crack of dawn, and I mean crack of dawn. I don't know the time, but it was pitch black. And we were standing in water up to our knees, digging ditches under the the Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were miserable. They were-- they were not-- they were not slapping you. They were not like the SS. And you were not in the psychological warfare-- is the gas coming, or is the water coming? You-- you started digging the ditches in the morning. You finished at nighttime. And-- and then you went to the barn, and you got a piece of bread and some water-- hot water something-- and you went to your bunk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was the minimum of-- of these appells, and the commandant was visible. And there was a prisoner who stole from another prisoner, and she was punished by the commandant because he felt that that shouldn't happen. Well, it shouldn't happen. And of course, I don't know what was going on. We were together-- mother and my sister and myself-- and we made sure that the piece of bread be shared between the three of us, and so on. And we stayed there. We were there through Christmas. That was our calendar. And I might sign-- sound very matter of fact to you, but believe me, the tears are gone out of my soul. I'm telling this story because I feel I'm telling it for the ones who are dead, and this is my contribution to the history, which I will be a part of one day. But I'm not crying anymore. I am sounding very cold maybe. I don't know. That was Christmas, and that was our calendar-- jumping, I am. And beginning of Christmas-- the beginning of January, we could already hear the-- the front coming closer to us. And again, we could hear the-- the cannons or whatever they were using. We knew it was the Russians. That-- we knew. I don't know how they knew it, but they knew it. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all of a sudden, the commandant must have got his orders from the SS. And we were evacuating by foot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One night, we just were assembled, and we walked. And we joined early in the morning. So we must have got sometime in the middle of the night. We started walking, and then it was early morning because I remember the sight of the people we joined-- well, if you can call it people. It was the death march, which has got its name now. We didn't know it then. It was a line-- a black line of something, which was moving very slowly as far as you could see and as far as you can see behind you. You continued-- we were sort of pushed into the line-- the ones from-- from Kurzbach. And you put a foot in front of the other foot, and you kept on going. If you fell down-- we are, again, under the SS-- and they are very, very visible. I mean, they are visible and cruel-- terribly, terribly cruel-- slapping, kicking, shooting. If a person [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] couldn't-- I am not even saying couldn't walk-- if it had just tripped, they shot him right then, let them lying there, and you continued. You didn't dare to find out if the person was dead or not. Some people were thinking that they are going to escape, and I applauded them at first in my soul. But as soon as they stepped out of the line, they were shot. So that was no escape of any kind. So we kept on walking. We kept on walking. And if you had to go to the bathroom, well, unfortunately, as primitive and as awful as it sounds, you did go into your pants, and you kept on walking. Mother and my sister and myself-- we walked, and what we did was we put one into the middle and-- and took the one who was in the middle under the armpits. And the one in the middle, then, was sort of being dragged by the two others, and you could close your eyes and sort of doze off. I mean, the three of us rotated, but I think I was the one who got most benefit of this protection because I just couldn't stay awake. Then we were housed overnight in one-- in a barn, which had an awful lot of hay. And we finally could lie down-- it didn't matter where and how-- and fall asleep. And there was a whole group of people-- of women. I mean, now, I don't remember any men around. Maybe they were, but the SS were so predominant and so terribly, terribly cruel. I mean, that shooting and-- and disposing of people's life-- lives-- in front of your eyes is something to see at the age of not quite-- no, I was 14 by then. And there was a group of women who were in a much better physical shape than we were. And they took my mother into their confidence and told her that, now that there is such a tremendous panic and-- and disorganization, that this is the only chance to cover yourself up in the hay in the morning and let the march continue and stay behind so that you-- that the front is right behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were two possibilities, and my mother was a very cautious person. She was not-- she-- she felt that, number one, they will shoot into the hay. Number two, she said she will-- they will take the big forks and go through the hay. Number three, she said it's the Russians who are coming, and what are the Russians going to know? They won't know if it is-- if we are-- if the Germans or no-- or not. And if we are not in a proper group and not identified, they will shoot us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was really petrified to make the decision to stay. And we did not stay. The women did. There was a group of women which stayed, which I will come later to. But they did, but we continued. And we continued this march, and we finished in Gross Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I cannot tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately, it must have been end of January, maybe beginning of February, but I am not really sure because Gross Rosen was just a pit stop because there was a complete hysteria of the Germans, believe it or not, of disposing of us. And I don't know what kind of disposals they had in Gross Rosen for in their-- in their category because I didn't even see properly Gross Rosen. All I know is that we got there. We stayed one night in some kind of a very overcrowded halls. There were people lying on top of me, and I was lying on top of somebody else. But at that point, I was glad I was sleeping. And the next day, we were put onto open cars-- cattle cars, but open. And we were shipped out of Gross Rosen. Now, when exactly it was-- it was terribly cold. I know we got some food that night in Gross Rosen. That I know. What, don't ask me because I wouldn't know. But now we are out in this open car-- cars. And it was a fairly long train. We were really taken there in a real panic now by this SS, and who didn't run fast enough was shot. And who didn't walk enough was shot. Everything was done in a complete hysteria-- and-- on their part. We didn't have anything left in us. We wouldn't be hysterical or not hysterical. We were just doing-- surviving-- whatever was in front of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said it was very cold. What were you wearing at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, we-- we did not have coats. We had some kind of pants with some kind of shoes, which didn't have bottoms. We had some kind of a pullover and-- and-- and then some kind of shawls, which became like rags we kept on wrapping ourselves in. But we were cold. We were terribly, terribly cold, I mean, cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we were on this train, huddled-- really huddled for warmth mainly-- well, mainly because there were so many of us. It was open, and we were huddled together to give each other some warmth. Not that we knew each other-- we didn't. And people were dying in the-- and we were going. And it was obvious they didn't know what to do with us or where to take us. And this train was going up and down and-- and stopping and then going. Some people, when it stopped, started jumping out of the car. And of course, they were shot because the-- the-- the SS was there. I don't know. They must have had a some kind of a caboose in between or something. But they were very much in evidence. And if the train stopped, they were paroling it and-- patrolling it-- and so forth. They were there. And when the people died in the-- in the wagons-- it sounds terrible, and it's true-- we threw the dead body out because we had more room. And so we continued. At one station-- and I feel it was Hanover, I don't know-- we stopped because there was an air raid. And we knew-- we saw them in the sky. And oh, were we thrilled. That was the first emotion I ever saw on anybody's face. There were the planes. And we started waving to them, hey, come and save us. Here we are. But of course, they thought from way above there-- they thought that was the movement of the troops, and they started actually bombarding the train. So a few more deaths, a few more dead bodies thrown out, and we continued. And we finished in a wooded area in the middle of nowhere. And we were taken out of the cart and taken into a truck-- open truck, standing, and driven, and-- not a long way. And we finished in Bergen-Belsen. And when I saw Bergen-Belsen, I know-- I knew in my heart that this is the end of the road. It was a stinking, filthy, dirty, muddy, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] godforsaken place with little huts with human beings, which stopped being human beings for eternity. And there we were. They took us off the truck and housed us. Again, it was overcrowded, of course. And you were-- I don't know where the mud came from-- but you were in mud over your ankles. And it was a stinking mud. Maybe it was the latrines. I don't know. I really don't know. And mother and myself and my sister-- we were put into a-- into a shack, where we were put into a, again, a middle bunk with now three other people. Now, the bunk is a single bunk, and there are six people lying in the bunk. And excuse the expression, but everybody's got a diarrhea, dysentery. Everybody stinks to blue heaven. I mean, the stink is overpowering. Not that we were so dainty anymore. But now we know that this is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.
